title: Voivodeships of Poland

infobox.value: 9,413 km2 (3,634.2 mi2) ([Opole](/wiki/Opole_Voivodeship "Opole Voivodeship")) – 35,580 km2 (13,737 mi2) ([Masovian](/wiki/Masovian_Voivodeship "Masovian Voivodeship"))

([/ˈvɔɪvoʊdʃɪp/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*VOY-vohd-ship*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), or **voivodship** ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *województwo* [[vɔjɛˈvut͡stfɔ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-wojew%C3%B3dztwo.ogg "File:Pl-województwo.ogg"); plural: *województwa* [[vɔjɛˈvut͡stfa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish")), is the highest-level [administrative division](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Poland "Administrative divisions of Poland") of [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), corresponding to a [province](/wiki/Province "Province") in many other countries. The term has been in use since the 14th century and is commonly translated into English as "**province**".[[1]](#cite_note-1)

A **voivodeship** ([/ˈvɔɪvoʊdʃɪp/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*VOY-vohd-ship*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), or **voivodship** ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *województwo* [[vɔjɛˈvut͡stfɔ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-wojew%C3%B3dztwo.ogg "File:Pl-województwo.ogg"); plural: *województwa* [[vɔjɛˈvut͡stfa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish")), is the highest-level [administrative division](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Poland "Administrative divisions of Poland") of [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), corresponding to a [province](/wiki/Province "Province") in many other countries. The term has been in use since the 14th century and is commonly translated into English as "**province**".[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The [Polish local government reforms](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Poland "Administrative divisions of Poland") adopted in 1998, which went into effect on 1 January 1999, reduced the number of voivodeships to sixteen. These 16 replaced the 49 [former voivodeships](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_the_Polish_People%27s_Republic "Subdivisions of the Polish People's Republic") that had existed from 1 July 1975, and bear a greater resemblance (in territory, but not in name) to the voivodeships that existed between 1950 and 1975.

Today's voivodeships are mostly named after historical and geographical regions, while those prior to 1998 generally took their names from the cities on which they were centered. The new units range in area from under 10,000 km2 (3,900 sq mi) ([Opole Voivodeship](/wiki/Opole_Voivodeship "Opole Voivodeship")) to over 35,000 km2 (14,000 sq mi) ([Masovian Voivodeship](/wiki/Masovian_Voivodeship "Masovian Voivodeship")), and in population from nearly one million (Opole Voivodeship) to over five million (Masovian Voivodeship).

Administrative authority at the voivodeship level is shared between a government-appointed governor called a [voivode](/wiki/Voivodes_of_Poland_(since_1999) "Voivodes of Poland (since 1999)") (*wojewoda*), an elected assembly called a *[sejmik](/wiki/Voivodeship_sejmik "Voivodeship sejmik")*, and an [executive board](/wiki/Voivodeship_executive_board "Voivodeship executive board") (*zarząd województwa*) chosen by that assembly, headed by a [voivodeship marshal](/wiki/Voivodeship_marshal "Voivodeship marshal") (*marszałek województwa*). Voivodeships are further divided into *[powiats](/wiki/Powiat "Powiat")* ('counties') and *[gminas](/wiki/Gmina "Gmina")* ('communes' or 'municipalities'), the smallest [administrative divisions of Poland](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Poland "Administrative divisions of Poland").

## Etymology and use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Voivodeships_of_Poland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and use")]

Some English-language sources, in historical contexts, speak of "[palatinates](/wiki/Count_palatine "Count palatine")" rather than "voivodeships". The term "[palatinate](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/palatinate "wikt:palatinate")" traces back to the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *palatinus*, which traces back to *palatium* ("palace").

More commonly used now is *province* or *voivodeship*. The latter is a [loanword](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword")-[calque](/wiki/Calque "Calque") [hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_word "Hybrid word") formed on the Polish "*województwo*".

Some writers argue against rendering *województwo* in English as "province", on historical grounds: before the third, last [Partition of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"), in 1795, each of the main constituent *regions* of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth")—[Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland"), [Lesser Poland](/wiki/Lesser_Poland "Lesser Poland"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), and [Royal Prussia](/wiki/Royal_Prussia "Royal Prussia")—was sometimes [idiosyncratically](/wiki/Idiosyncrasy "Idiosyncrasy") referred to as a "province" (*[prowincja](/wiki/Prowincja "Prowincja")*). According to the argument, such a *prowincja* (for example, Greater Poland) cannot consist of a number of subdivisions ("*województw**a***", the plural of "*województw**o***") that are likewise called "provinces". This, however, is an antiquarian consideration, as the word "province" has not been used in Poland in this sense of a *region* for over two centuries; and those former larger political units, all now obsolete, can now be referred to in English as what they actually were: "regions".

The [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") *województwo*, designating a second-tier Polish or Polish–Lithuanian administrative unit, derives from *wojewoda*, (etymologically, a '[warlord](/wiki/Warlord "Warlord")', 'war leader' or 'leader of warriors',[[2]](#cite_note-2) giving it the same etymology as the English word "Duchy", but now simply the [governor](/wiki/Governor "Governor") of a *województwo*) and the [suffix](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") *-ztwo* (a "state or condition").

The English *voivodeship*, which is a [hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_word "Hybrid word") of the [loanword](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") *voivode* and *-ship* (the latter a [suffix](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") that [calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque") the Polish suffix *-ztwo*), has never been much used and is absent from many dictionaries. According to the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, it first appeared in 1792, spelt "woiwodship", in the sense of "the district or province governed by a voivode." The word subsequently appeared in 1886 also in the sense of "the office or dignity of a voivode."[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Poland's Commission on Standardization of Geographic Names outside the Republic of Poland prefers the form which omits the 'e', recommending the spelling "voivodship", for use in English.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Administrative powers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Voivodeships_of_Poland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Administrative powers")]

Competences and powers at voivodeship level are shared between the [voivode](/wiki/Voivodes_of_Poland_(since_1999) "Voivodes of Poland (since 1999)") (governor), the [sejmik](/wiki/Voivodeship_sejmik "Voivodeship sejmik") (regional assembly) and the [marshal](/wiki/Voivodeship_marshal "Voivodeship marshal"). In most cases these institutions are all based in one city, but in [Kuyavian-Pomeranian](/wiki/Kuyavian-Pomeranian_Voivodeship "Kuyavian-Pomeranian Voivodeship") and [Lubusz Voivodeship](/wiki/Lubusz_Voivodeship "Lubusz Voivodeship") the voivode's offices are in a different city from those of the executive and the sejmik. Voivodeship capitals are listed in the table below.

The *voivode* is appointed by the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Poland "Prime Minister of Poland") and is the regional representative of the central government. The voivode acts as the head of central government institutions at regional level (such as the police and fire services, passport offices, and various inspectorates), manages central government property in the region, oversees the functioning of local government, coordinates actions in the field of public safety and environment protection, and exercises special powers in emergencies. The voivode's offices collectively are known as the *urząd wojewódzki*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The *sejmik* is elected every five years. (The first of the five-year terms began in 2018; previous terms lasted four years.)[[7]](#cite_note-7)) [Elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Poland "Elections in Poland") for the sejmik fall at the same time as that of local authorities at *[powiat](/wiki/Powiat "Powiat")* and *[gmina](/wiki/Gmina "Gmina")* level. The sejmik passes [by-laws](/wiki/By-law "By-law"), including the voivodeship's development strategies and budget. It also elects the *marszałek* and other members of the executive, and holds them to account.

The *[executive](/wiki/Voivodeship_executive_board "Voivodeship executive board")* (*zarząd województwa*), headed by the marszałek drafts the budget and development strategies, implements the resolutions of the sejmik, manages the voivodeship's property, and deals with many aspects of regional policy, including management of [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") funding. The marshal's offices are collectively known as the *urząd marszałkowski*.

### Economies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Voivodeships_of_Poland&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Economies")]

Main article: [List of Polish voivodeships by GRP](/wiki/List_of_Polish_voivodeships_by_GRP "List of Polish voivodeships by GRP")

According to 2017 Eurostat data, the GDP per capita of Polish voivodeships varies notably and there is a large gap between the richest per capita voivodeship (being the Masovian Voivodeship at 33,500 EUR) and the poorest per capita (being the Lublin Voivodeship at 14,400 EUR).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Polish People's Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Voivodeships_of_Poland&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Polish People's Republic")]

After World War II, the new administrative division of the country within the new national borders was based on the prewar one and included 14 (+2) voivodeships, then 17 (+5). The voivodeships in the east that had not been [annexed by the Soviet Union](/wiki/Territories_of_Poland_annexed_by_the_Soviet_Union "Territories of Poland annexed by the Soviet Union") had their borders left almost unchanged. The [newly acquired territories](/wiki/Recovered_Territories "Recovered Territories") in the west and north were organized into the new voivodeships of [Szczecin](/wiki/Szczecin_Voivodeship_(1946%E2%80%931975) "Szczecin Voivodeship (1946–1975)"), [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw_Voivodeship "Wrocław Voivodeship") and [Olsztyn](/wiki/Olsztyn_Voivodeship "Olsztyn Voivodeship"), and partly joined to [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk_Voivodeship_(1945%E2%80%931975) "Gdańsk Voivodeship (1945–1975)"), [Katowice](/wiki/Katowice_Voivodeship "Katowice Voivodeship") and [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84_Voivodeship "Poznań Voivodeship") voivodeships. Two cities were granted voivodeship status: [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") and [Łódź](/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA "Łódź").

In 1950, new voivodeships were created: [Koszalin](/wiki/Koszalin_Voivodeship_(1950%E2%80%931975) "Koszalin Voivodeship (1950–1975)") (previously part of [Szczecin](/wiki/Szczecin_Voivodeship_(1946%E2%80%931975) "Szczecin Voivodeship (1946–1975)")), [Opole](/wiki/Opole_Voivodeship "Opole Voivodeship") (previously part of [Katowice](/wiki/Katowice_Voivodeship "Katowice Voivodeship")), and [Zielona Góra](/wiki/Zielona_G%C3%B3ra_Voivodeship_(1975%E2%80%931998) "Zielona Góra Voivodeship (1975–1998)") (previously part of [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84_Voivodeship "Poznań Voivodeship"), [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw_Voivodeship_(1975%E2%80%931998) "Wrocław Voivodeship (1975–1998)") and [Szczecin](/wiki/Szczecin_Voivodeship_(1946%E2%80%931975) "Szczecin Voivodeship (1946–1975)") voivodeships). In 1957, three more cities were granted voivodeship status: [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"), [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") and [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań").

**Poland's voivodeships 1975–1998**

See also: [Subdivisions of the Polish People's Republic](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_the_Polish_People%27s_Republic "Subdivisions of the Polish People's Republic") and [Voivodeships of Poland (1975–1998)](/wiki/Voivodeships_of_Poland_(1975%E2%80%931998) "Voivodeships of Poland (1975–1998)")

Administrative division of Poland between 1979 and 1998 included 49 voivodeships upheld after the establishment of the [Third Polish Republic](/wiki/Third_Polish_Republic "Third Polish Republic") in 1989 for another decade. This reorganisation of the administrative division of Poland was mainly a result of the local government reform acts of 1973–1975. In place of the three-level administrative division (voivodeship, county, commune), a new two-level administrative division was introduced (49 small voivodeships, and communes). The three smallest voivodeships—[Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") and [Łódź](/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA "Łódź")—had the special status of municipal voivodeship; the city president (mayor) was also provincial governor.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Voivodeships_of_Poland&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** The word [voivodeship](/wiki/Voivodeship "Voivodeship"), as an equivalent for *województwo*, appears in some large English dictionaries such as the [OED](/wiki/OED "OED") and [Webster's Third New International Dictionary](/wiki/Webster%27s_Third_New_International_Dictionary "Webster's Third New International Dictionary") but is not in common [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") usage. Hence the word *province* is a recommended translation: "*Jednostki podziału administracyjnego Polski tłumaczymy tak: województwo*—province..." ("Polish administrative units are translated as follows: *województwo*—[province](/wiki/Province "Province")..."). Arkadiusz Belczyk, [*"Tłumaczenie polskich nazw geograficznych na język angielski"*](https://web.archive.org/web/20160303203627/http://serwistlumacza.com/content/view/27/32/) ("[Translation](/wiki/Translation "Translation") of Polish Geographical Names into English"), 2002-2006. Examples: [New Provinces of Poland (1998)](http://www.staff.amu.edu.pl/~zbzw/ph/pro/plpro.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20110608133233/http://www.staff.amu.edu.pl/~zbzw/ph/pro/plpro.html) 2011-06-08 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), [Map of Poland](http://www.map-of-Poland.co.uk/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20110426190140/http://www.map-of-poland.co.uk/) 26 April 2011 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), [English names of Polish provinces](https://web.archive.org/web/20110716043017/http://serwistlumacza.com/content/view/78/). More examples:
   * "Following the reform of the administrative structure in 1973-1975, the number of provinces (*województwo*) was increased from 22 to 49... [I]ncreasing the number of *provinces* meant the reduction of each in size. In this way Warsaw was able to dilute the political importance of the *provincial* party chiefs." "Poland", *[The Encyclopedia Americana](/wiki/The_Encyclopedia_Americana "The Encyclopedia Americana")*, 1986, volume 22, p. 312.
   * "Poland is divided into 49 *provinces*." "Poland", *[The Columbia Encyclopedia](/wiki/The_Columbia_Encyclopedia "The Columbia Encyclopedia")*, sixth edition, edited by Paul Lagassé, [Columbia University Press](/wiki/Columbia_University_Press "Columbia University Press"), 2000, p. 2256.
   * "Local government in Poland is organized on three levels. The largest units, at the regional level, are the *województwa* ('provinces')..." "Poland", *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")*, 15th edition, 2010, *[Macropaedia](/wiki/Macropaedia "Macropaedia")*, volume 25, p. 937.
   * "GOVERNMENT... Administrative divisions: 16 *provinces* (*województwa*, singular–*województwo*)..." "Poland," in [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency"), *[The CIA World Factbook](/wiki/The_CIA_World_Factbook "The CIA World Factbook") 2010*, New York, Skyhorse Publishing, Inc., 2009, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781602397279](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781602397279 "Special:BookSources/9781602397279"), p. 546. The same information appears in the current online [*CIA World Factbook* / "Poland / Administrative divisions".](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/poland/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20210730084739/https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/poland/) 30 July 2021 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") In this source, where "English translations" of province names are given, they are in the [noun](/wiki/Noun "Noun") ("Silesia"), not the [adjective](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") ("Silesia***n***"), form.
   * Professor Paul Best, of [Southern Connecticut State University](/wiki/Southern_Connecticut_State_University "Southern Connecticut State University"), writes: "[I]n standard dictionaries the Polish word *województwo* is translated as 'province'." Paul Best, review of Bogdan Horbal, *Lemko Studies: A Handbook* (2010), in *[The Polish Review](/wiki/The_Polish_Review "The Polish Review")*, vol. 58, no. 4 (2013), pp. 125–26.
